data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-03T00:54:04.446Z</current_datetime>

I'm migrating a .NET 10 web API to .NET 11. The app uses EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider.
We have a repository pattern where some methods use synchronous LINQ:

```csharp
public class ProductRepository
{
    private readonly CosmosDbContext _context;

    public List<Product> GetAll()
    {
        return _context.Products.ToList();
    }

    public Product GetById(int id)
    {
        return _context.Products.First(p => p.Id == id);
    }

    public int GetCount()
    {
        return _context.Products.Count();
    }

    public void Save(Product product)
    {
        _context.Products.Add(product);
        _context.SaveChanges();
    }
}
```

In .NET 10 we had the sync-over-async opt-in configured. What breaks in .NET 11?

data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about what breaks when migrating from .NET 10 to .NET 11 with EF Core using the Azure Cosmos DB provider, specifically regarding synchronous LINQ operations.

This is about a known breaking change in EF Core. Let me recall what I know:

In EF Core with the Azure Cosmos DB provider, synchronous operations were never natively supported. The Cosmos DB SDK is async-only. In earlier versions, EF Core allowed "sync-over-async" — it would block on async calls internally. This was always problematic (potential deadlocks, thread pool starvation).

In EF Core 10 (.NET 10), they likely made this opt-in or started warning about it. 

In .NET 11, the breaking change is that synchronous operations on the Cosmos DB provider now throw an `InvalidOperationException` by default, and the configuration option to allow sync-over-async was completely removed. Looking at the EF Core timeline, the Cosmos DB provider received major updates in version 9, had sync-over-async available as an opt-in feature in version 10, and then removed that capability entirely in version 11. In .NET 11, that opt-in is completely gone, so any synchronous LINQ operations like `ToList()`, `First()`, `Count()`, and `SaveChanges()` will immediately throw an `InvalidOperationException`. The solution is straightforward: I need to convert all these calls to their async counterparts and make the containing methods async as well.
